coredata.dc:title: Numerical assessment of hydrothermal and entropy generation characteristics of graphene quantum dots nanofluid in a microchannel heat sink incorporating secondary channels and ribs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Souby, M. Mohamed
Bargal, Mohamed H.S.
Maher, Hisham
Salman, Mohammad
Liu, Jie
Kim, Sung Chul
coredata.dc:description: Efficient heat dissipation from electronic chips is essential for enhancing their performance and longevity. Significant progress has been achieved through the introduction of nanofluids and microchannel heat sinks. However, there is still a need for nanofluids that demonstrate exceptional thermal properties and stability. Graphene quantum dots nanofluid, an innovative carbon-based coolant with zero-dimensional nanostructures, presents promising features such as excellent chemical stability, surfactant-free preparation, superior thermal properties, and minimal impact on rheological characteristics. This study explores, for the first time, the hydrothermal behavior and entropy generation of graphene quantum dots nanofluid in a microchannel heat sink comprising secondary flow channels and ribs. To this end, a three-dimensional conjugate heat transfer model is built using ANSYS Fluent software, and the governing equations are solved using the finite volume method. The simulations are carried out considering temperature-dependent thermophysical properties under different concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.5 % and Reynolds numbers ranging from 100 to 500. The results reveal that the maximum bottom wall surface temperature decreases by 5.88 K, while the heat transfer coefficient improves by 24.9 % compared to the base fluid. Moreover, the total entropy generation reduces by 14.7 %. On the other hand, the pressure drop increases by 43 %. Overall, the highest increment in Performance Evaluation Criteria is 10.8 % at a Reynolds number of 100 and a concentration of 0.5 %, making this particular condition suitable for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electronics cooling
Microchannel heat sink
Hydrothermal performance
Graphene quantum dots nanofluid
Secondary channels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Configuration of the MCHS under investigation: (a) 3-D isometric view; (b) computational domain.
Flow pathlines colored by velocity in one part of the MCHS at Z = 0.35 mm for different ϕ and Re = 500.
Variation of h with Re and ϕ.

(a) Variation of Tmax with Re at different values of ϕ; (b) Temperature distribution with isoclip higher than 300K along the bottom surface under different Re, and ϕ, ; (c) variation of ΔT with Re, and ϕ.
Dependency of (a) ϴ; (b) R on ϕ at different Re.
Effect of Re and ϕ on (a) pressure drop; (b) pumping power; (c) pressure drop distribution along the microchannel; (d) PEC.
Variation of (a) thermal entropy generation; (b) frictional entropy generation; (c) total entropy generation with Re and ϕ.
(a) Be for different Re, and ϕ; (b) Contribution of the heat exchange and friction to the total entropy generation.
Detailed dimensions of the MCHS under investigation.
Empirical equations of the thermal conductivity and viscosity for the GQDs NF [41,43].
Variation of Tmean with Re and ϕ.

head.ce:title: Numerical assessment of hydrothermal and entropy generation characteristics of graphene quantum dots nanofluid in a microchannel heat sink incorporating secondary channels and ribs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Conversion Lab, Korea Institute of Energy Research, Daejeon, 34129, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy Conversion Lab, Korea Institute of Energy Research, Daejeon, 34129, South korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient heat dissipation from electronic chips is essential for enhancing their performance and longevity. Significant progress has been achieved through the introduction of nanofluids and microchannel heat sinks. However, there is still a need for nanofluids that demonstrate exceptional thermal properties and stability. Graphene quantum dots nanofluid, an innovative carbon-based coolant with zero-dimensional nanostructures, presents promising features such as excellent chemical stability, surfactant-free preparation, superior thermal properties, and minimal impact on rheological characteristics. This study explores, for the first time, the hydrothermal behavior and entropy generation of graphene quantum dots nanofluid in a microchannel heat sink comprising secondary flow channels and ribs. To this end, a three-dimensional conjugate heat transfer model is built using ANSYS Fluent software, and the governing equations are solved using the finite volume method. The simulations are carried out considering temperature-dependent thermophysical properties under different concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.5 % and Reynolds numbers ranging from 100 to 500. The results reveal that the maximum bottom wall surface temperature decreases by 5.88 K, while the heat transfer coefficient improves by 24.9 % compared to the base fluid. Moreover, the total entropy generation reduces by 14.7 %. On the other hand, the pressure drop increases by 43 %. Overall, the highest increment in Performance Evaluation Criteria is 10.8 % at a Reynolds number of 100 and a concentration of 0.5 %, making this particular condition suitable for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Emerging modern technology in the recent industrial revolution facilitates the expansion of industry scale, which positively impacts economies and industries worldwide. These technologies involve using computers for automatic control, networking, system integration, and acquiring data via the internet. Most cloud computing setups deliver services via data centers operated by servers. These centers utilize specialized computing hardware and software designed specifically for cloud services, such as multi-core semiconductor processors and operating systems optimized for cloud usage [1]. The advancement of high-performance servers is significantly influenced by efficient thermal management, given that semiconductor chips can release up to 150 W of heat. Effective cooling solutions are becoming increasingly crucial for further technological progress. Typically, the standard operational temperature for central processing units (CPUs), graphics processing units (GPUs), and other chips remains below 70 °C. Chips' reliability decreases by 10 % for every 2 °C rise above this standard operating temperature [2]. The consistent generation of heat from components has underscored the need for efficient heat dissipation strategies. Conventional approaches like air cooling struggle to manage the considerable heat generated adequately. To tackle this challenge, various cooling techniques, including micro-heat pipes, micro-jet impingement, micro electro-hydrodynamics, and microchannel heat sinks (MCHSs), have been developed to address this issue [3]. Among these options, MCHSs stand out as the most favorable choice due to their lightweight design, high heat dissipation capability, impressive surface-to-volume ratio, and superior cooling capacity [4]. In 1981, Tuckerman and Pease [5]. introduced MCHSs to address cooling issues in high-density electronic systems. Their concept involved using a network of silicon substrate comprising tiny channels to create a compact and effective cooling mechanism. According to their results, their microchannels could effectively disperse heat flux up to approximately 7.9 × 103 kW/m2. Since the adoption of MCHSs, extensive research has been conducted to assess the heat transfer characteristics and pressure drop. Numerous scholars have focused their efforts on investigating and enhancing the performance of MCHSs to increase heat transfer rates while reducing power consumption [6–9]. Improving MCHS performance has done by two approaches. One approach involves modifying the shape, arrangement, or size of the microchannels [10–12]. By optimizing the geometric pattern, researchers aim to create more efficient pathways for heat dissipation, leading to better thermal performance [13]. The other approach involves using improved cooling fluids, such as advanced fluids (Nanofluids) with higher thermal properties like thermal conductivity [14–16]. Nanofluids (NFs), which involve suspending nanoparticles into conventional cooling fluids, offer a promising solution to enhance heat transfer [17,18]. These fluids can significantly reduce the thermal boundary layer thickness, eventually boosting convective heat transfer coefficient (h) and consequently increasing the heat transfer rate in MCHSs [19,20]. These two approaches provide alternative strategies for optimizing heat exchange of MCHSs, depending on the specific requirements and constraints of the cooling system [19,21–24]. Karimipour et al. [25] conducted a numerical investigation of indentation impact on a laminar flow and heat transfer behavior of NFs composed of Silver particles suspended in water across rectangular microchannel. Greater temperature drops were associated with rising Reynolds numbers (Re), number of indentations, and higher nanoparticle concentration. Additionally, the speed and dimensionless temperature at the center line were incremented by the indentation in the microchannel. Wang et al. [26] conducted a numerical assessment of the effect of secondary flow structure on the performance of a hierarchical MCHS operated with Fe3O4 aqueous nanofluid. The hierarchical MCHS, featuring a trapezoidal secondary channel, consistently exhibited drag reduction effects within Re = 200–600. Moreover, the pressure drop was observed to reduce by 2.46 %, highlighting its notable ability to reduce drag. Furthermore, the incorporation of a trapezoidal secondary flow in the MCHS resulted in a notable increase of 9.70 % in the Nusselt number (Nu). Introducing ribs plays a pivotal role in directing flow dynamics toward secondary channels, thereby facilitating and improving flow mixing processes. Simultaneously, the incorporation of secondary channels serves a dual purpose, enhancing flow mixing initiated by the ribs and contributing to an expanded flow area. This dual action minimizes pressure drop caused by the presence of ribs [27–29]. Therefore, Akbari et al. [30] assessed a role of ribs on flow and heat transfer characteristics with water/aluminum oxide NF in rectangular microchannel. The findings demonstrated that ribs in microchannels enhanced the heat transfer efficiency and reduced the heat gradient between layers fluids with high pressure drop as a penalty. Nu was also enhanced by 4.5 times with using water/aluminum oxide NF and ribs in the microchannel compared to the smooth microchannel with water. Additionally, Gravndyana et al. [31] numerically studied an impact of aspect ratios (AR) of rib in a two-dimensional microchannel with the thermal performance of TiO2 nanofluids. The AR of ribs were changed as follows; 10,15, 20 and 25. The results showed that the best performance evaluation criterion was with 10 of AR, 300 of Re, and 0.04 % of nanoparticles concentration (ϕ). MCHSs that equipped with both ribs and secondary channels provide superior heat transfer improvement [27,32,33]. Ghani et al. [27] conducted a numerical study on water cooled MCHS with secondary oblique channels and rectangular ribs (MC-SOCRR) for Re ranging from 100 to 500. They found that MC-SOCRR outperformed designs with only rectangular ribs or only secondary oblique channels due to its larger flow area and enhanced flow mixing. Optimal performance, with a secondary channel width ratio of 0.666, rib width ratio of 0.5, and channel angle of 45°, achieved a performance factor of 1.98 at Re = 500. Bahiraei et al. [34] investigated the second law analysis of graphene–silver hybrid NF in a MCHS integrated with ribs and secondary channels across a broad range of Re and ϕ. The outcomes revealed that increasing Re and ϕ led to a reduction in total entropy generation. Specifically, a 7 % decrease in entropy generation was observed at ϕ = 0.1 % and Re = 300, while a 17 % reduction occurred at ϕ = 0.06 % as Re increased from 100 to 500. In another study, Bahiraei et al. [28] examined the efficiency of graphene-silver hybrid NF in an MCHS featuring ribs and secondary channels. The study examined how varying Re and ϕ influenced system effectiveness. Results showed that higher Re and ϕ values reduced MCHS temperature and increased h. The maximum enhancements observed were a 17 % increase in h and a 3.42 K reduction in MCHS temperature. However, elevated pumping power was noted with higher ϕ and Re values. Jamshidmofid et al. [3] studied, the thermohydraulic characteristics of a hybrid NF with an rGO-Co3O4 nanocomposite in an MCHS with rectangular ribs and sinusoidal cavities. The simulation involved ϕ of 0.05 %, 0.1 %, and 0.2 % across Re ranging from 100 to 500. Their findings indicated that using 0.2 % NF at Re = 500 enhanced thermal characteristics with minimal pressure drop increment. Consequently, they recommended this scenario for practical applications.

Graphene, a carbon-based material composed of a single-atom-thick sheet of hexagonally shaped carbon atoms, stands out due to its macroscale transverse size, which grants it a thermal conductivity approximately 5000 times greater than that of water [35]. However, while graphene's exceptional thermal conductivity makes it an attractive choice for use in NFs, its significant transverse dimensions hinder both Brownian motion and stability within the working fluid. To address this issue, researchers have suggested employing surfactants during the preparation of graphene NFs. Although surfactants enhance the stability of additive phases, they also impact the thermal properties of NFs, resulting in a reduction in their thermal conductivity [35,36]. Alternatively, a different strategy involves reducing graphene's cross dimensions to the nanoscale. This approach improves Brownian motion and stabilizes graphene within the base fluid, eliminating the necessity for surfactants. The resulting structure, known as Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), is a novel development obtained through this dimension-reduction process [37–42]. To date, there has been limited research focused on the preparation and application of NFs incorporating GQDs. Ettefaghi et al. [40] synthesized a GQDs NF for implementation in radiators, achieving a 5.2 % and 17 % enhancement in thermal conductivity and h for a 100 ppm concentration. Sedaghat and Yousefi [41]. successfully created a GQDs NF using various base fluids and observed notable enhancements in thermal conductivity. They reported improvements of 53 %, 21 %, and 18 % respectively upon incorporating 0.5 % GQDs to water, ethylene glycol, and a water–ethylene glycol mixture, respectively. Soleymaniha et al. [42] prepared an aqueous GQDs NF for use in industrial heat pipes and thermosyphons. The water-based GQDs NFs exhibited superior characteristics such as absence sedimentation, high thermal conductivity, and minimal impact on viscosity. The characteristics of GQDs NF in a microchannel heat exchanger under varied concentrations and Re were numerically studied by Jamshidmofid et al. [43]. Their findings indicated that at Re values of 50 and 100, incorporating the NF significantly enhances heat transfer rates with only a marginal increase in pressure drop. Moreover, it is recommended to use the GQDs NF at Re values of 50 and 100 to improve heat exchanger performance. However, under Re values of 150 and 200, the use of the NF is not advisable due to a significant increase in pressure drop.

Based on the existing literature, the novelty of the current study can be introduced as follows.
●
Until now, extensive research has focused on MCHSs using NFs, However, there is still a need for innovative NFs that demonstrate exceptional thermal properties and stability.
●
There has been limited investigations into the use of NFs in microchannels equipped with ribs, and secondary channels.
●
There are no published studies on the hydrothermal and entropy generation performances of GQDs NF in MCHSs.

The objective of this study is to numerically analyze the hydrothermal and entropy generation characteristics of a novel GQDs NF for the first time in the MCHS developed by Ghani et al. [27], which includes ribs and secondary channels. The properties of the GQDs NF are applied in a temperature-dependent manner based on experimental data to ensure accurate numerical results. The conjugate heat transfer model is solved using the finite volume method in ANSYS fluent software. The effects of ϕ, and Re on the maximum temperature, temperature uniformity, R, h, and pressure drop are explored. Additionally, thermal, frictional, and total entropy generations are analyzed. Future recommendations for the use of GQDs NF in MCHSs for cooling high heat flux electronics are provided.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Definition of the MCHS and nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: The MCHS under consideration comprises ten microchannels, incorporating rectangular-shaped ribs and secondary flow passages to enhance heat dissipation performance. In this MCHS, the GQDs NF flows through the microchannels, absorbing heat from the bottom. The ribs increase heat transfer, promote flow mixing, and guide the fluid toward the secondary channels. By disrupting the thermal boundary layer and increasing the heat transfer area, they significantly contribute to improved heat transfer. Additionally, the presence of secondary channels reduces pressure drop and enhances heat transfer by increasing the flow area. Fig. 1 (a) illustrates the configuration of the copper-made MCHS under investigation. Fig. 1 (b) depicts a schematic representation of a symmetric portion of the MCHS, considered as the computational domain, along with its associated geometric characteristics. The values of the geometrical parameters are detailed in.

Table 1 . Notably, based on results published by Ghani et al. [27], these geometric parameters were selected to minimize pressure drop while maximizing heat transfer. The GQDs NF with concentrations of 0 %, 0.1 %, 0.3 %, and 0.5 % are considered. GQDs are composed of single to ten-layered graphene sheets, with lateral dimensions smaller than 100 nm, exhibiting zero-dimensional (0D) characteristics. The primary feature of GQDs NFs lies in the inherent excellent chemical stability of GQDs nanoparticles, surpassing that of other nanoparticles. Moreover, the synthesis process does not involve the use of surfactants (environmentally friendly). This is advantageous because GQDs can be easily produced through a simple method at a relatively low cost. Another positive aspect of GQDs is their substantial surface area, resulting in enhanced thermal conductivity. The process of preparing the investigated GQDs NF is extensively explained in Ref. [41]. This process involves synthesizing GQDs through chemical vapor deposition using nitric acid and active carbon, followed by the production of NF with varying volume concentrations containing these synthesized particles. Additionally, UV–Vis spectroscopy confirmed the stability of the GQDs NF, which remained stable for an extended period.

To explore the behavior of the GQDs NF within the MCHS, the continuity, momentum, and energy equations are solved. The flow regime in this study is laminar, and the NF is considered incompressible, single-phase, and Newtonian. Since the domain was confined, gravitational effects were deemed insignificant, resulting in a consolidated forced-convection system. However, radiation and viscosity effects were regarded as negligible. Additionally, a steady-state assumption for both heat transfer and fluid flow is made. With these assumptions in mind, the governing equations, covering continuity, momentum, and energy, are as follows [6,44,45]:

Continuity equation:
(1)
∇ .
(
ρ
n f
u →
)
= 0

Momentum equation:
(2)
∇ .
(
ρ
n f
u →
u →
)
= − ∇ p + ∇ .
(
μ
n f
∇
u →
)

Energy equation for the fluid domain:
(3)
∇ .
(
ρ
n f
u →
c
p , n f
T
)
= ∇ .
(
k
n f
∇ T
)

Equation (4) represents the governing equation for the conduction heat transfer in solid walls. It is critical to understand that the solid components do not contain heat sources. In addition, it is assumed that the thermophysical characteristics of the solid walls remain constant.

Energy equation for the solid domain:
(4)
∇ .
(
k s
∇
T s
)
= 0
where the symbols
ρ
, k, μ, and cp denote density, thermal conductivity, viscosity, and specific heat, respectively. While
u →
, p, and T refer to the velocity, pressure, and temperature, respectively, and the subscripts f and s denote the fluid and solid, respectively.

The finite volume method is utilized for numerical simulation. The mass, momentum, and energy equations are solved using the second-order upwind method. Pressure-velocity coupling is implemented with the SIMPLE method. A convergence criterion of 10−6, uniformly applied to all variables, determines when to halt the numerical solution process. The boundary conditions applied to the computational domain are depicted in Fig. 2 . The microchannel's lower surface (z = 0) experiences a continuous heat flux of 106 W/m2. The top surface of the microchannel is adiabatic, while the lateral surfaces (y = 0 and y = L8) are subject to symmetrical boundary conditions. At the microchannel inlet (x = 0), uniform velocity and temperature profiles are assumed, and at (x = L), the pressure outlet condition is set to zero. A temperature of 293.15 K is specified at the inlet, and constant Reynolds numbers of 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 are used to calculate the inlet velocity. Additionally, the inner walls of the MCHS (fluid/solid interface) adhere to the no-slip boundary condition. It is noteworthy note that, the continuum assumption holds true when the mean free path of fluid molecules is significantly smaller than the flow length scale. Conversely, non-continuum effects, such as slip conditions, become significant when this condition is not met. The Knudsen number (Kn) quantifies the ratio of the mean free path of fluid molecules to the channel length scale. In the present study, water has a mean free path of approximately 0.3 nm under standard conditions, and the smallest flow dimension is 37.5 μm. Consequently, the Kn is below 10−3, indicating that the continuum approach based on the Navier-Stokes equations is applicable, and the no-slip boundary condition is valid.

The thermal conductivity and viscosity of the GQDs NF vary with temperature. Empirical correlations for these properties used in the numerical analysis are provided in Table 2 [41,43]. Density and specific heat of the NF are assumed to be constant. Density and specific heat values for the NF at a specific concentration are determined using Equations (5) and (6), respectively.
(5)
ρ
n f
= ϕ
ρ p
+
(
1 − ϕ
)
ρ f
(6)
(
ρ
c p
)
n f
= ϕ
(
ρ
c p
)
p
+
(
1 − ϕ
)
(
ρ
c p
)
f
where the symbol ϕ represents the volume fraction. The subscripts f and p are associated with the hosting fluid (water) and nanoparticles, respectively.

Mosaic meshing technology was used to discretize the computational domain. This approach accelerates meshing by reducing face count, creating higher quality cells, and demonstrating efficient parallel scalability. Importantly, Mosaic meshing enables polyhedral connections between different mesh types, enhancing versatility and adaptability for complex geometries and varied mesh structures. Additionally, this method yields accurate solutions with minimal elements [3,46]. Consequently, this leads to reduced computational time and cost. The selection of the element count in this figure follows the guiding principle of achieving the highest accuracy with the lowest computational cost. A systematic evaluation was conducted by monitoring the variation of the R and pressure drop for ϕ = 0 % and Re = 100 to determine the optimal mesh configuration. This involved gradually increasing the number of elements until the appropriate mesh was identified based on the analysis. Fig. 3 presents the findings of the investigation. The R and pressure drop exhibit negligible variations once the number of elements exceeds 756,049, according to the findings. Hence, this grid is selected for further simulations.

The current numerical approach is utilized to analyze the fluid flow and heat transfer of water in an MCHS equipped with secondary channels and ribs. This analysis aims to compare the mean bottom wall temperature with those reported by Ghani et al. [27], thereby evaluating the accuracy of the numerical method employed in this study. As depicted in Fig. 1, the current study considers the same geometry with its specific dimensions. Fig. 4 provides a detailed comparison of these results. The data points are closely clustered, showing minimal variation and a maximum error of less than 0.17 %. This consistency underscores the accuracy of the numerical approach utilized. Consequently, the current numerical method is deemed reliable and suitable for numerically addressing the fluid flow and heat transfer of the GQDs NF in the present MCHS.

The considered MCHS is attached to an electronic chip for the purpose of cooling. Uniform temperature distribution across the electronic chip surface is considered when assessing the effectiveness of MCHSs. Specifically, the cooling performance is evaluated by examining the temperature disparity between the maximum and minimum temperatures on the electronic component. In this context, the measure for assessing cooling uniformity (ϴ) can be expressed as [44]:
(7)
ϴ =
T max
−
T min
q
where Tmax and Tmin are the maximum and minimum temperatures of the electronic chip surface, respectively. Q is the heat flux generated by the electronic devices on the bottom surface of the MCHS. A lower ϴ signifies more temperature uniformity. However, relying solely on the temperature uniformity is inadequate for evaluating the effectiveness of an MCHS. Another crucial consideration is the average temperature of the electronic chip.

Thus, the subsequent formula is expressed as follows [44]:
(8)
R =
T mean
−
T in
q
where Tin is the inlet temperature, Tmean is the average temperature of the electronic chip, and R is the thermal resistance. The superior cooling effectiveness of the MCHS is indicated by a lower average surface temperature and a corresponding decrease in thermal resistance.

The convective heat transfer coefficient is obtained as follows [44]:
(9)
h =
q
T mean
−
T m
In this context,
T m
=
T in
+
T out
2
, where Tout is the coolant outlet temperature.

The pumping power is determined by using the following equation [44]:
(10)
W ˙
pump
=
V ˙
Δ P
where
V ˙
, indicates the volumetric flowrate, and
Δ P
denote pressure drop.

The performance evaluation Criterion (PEC) can be expressed as follows [28]:
(11)
PEC =
Nu /
Nu 0
(
f /
f 0
)
1 / 3
where Nu and f denote the Nusselt number and friction factor, respectively, with the subscript 0 indicating the hosting fluid.

Local entropy generation includes thermal (
S h ‴
) and frictional (
S f ‴
) terms, as follows [47]:
(12)
S h ‴
=
k nf
T 2
[
(
∂ T
∂ x
)

+
(
∂ T
∂ y
)

(

∂ z
)
2
]
(13)
S f ‴
=
μ nf
T
{
2

∂ u
∂ x
)

)

)

∂ z
+
∂ w
∂ x
)
2
+
(
∂ v
∂ z
+
∂ w
∂ y
)
2
}

The total local entropy generation rate (
S t ‴
) is expressed as [47]
(14)
S t ‴
=
S h ‴
+
S f ‴

Subsequently, the global entropy generation rates are computed as follows [47]:
(15)
S t
= ∫
S t ‴
dV ;
S h
= ∫
S h ‴
dV ;
S f
= ∫
S f ‴
dV

The Bejan number (Be) evaluates the individual impacts of the parameters on entropy generation. As shown in Equation (16), Be represents the ratio of thermal entropy production to total entropy production. Essentially, a high Be indicates that heat exchange contributes more significantly to entropy production compared to friction [46].
(16)
Be =
S h
S t

This study examines the hydrothermal performance of a new NF containing GQDs nanoparticles within an MCHS that incorporates both secondary channels and ribs. The study investigates the impact of Re within the range of 100–500 and ϕ ranging from 0 % to 0.5 % on various performance parameters. Specifically, the analysis focuses on evaluating the influence of these working conditions on the h, Tmax, Tmean, R, and ϴ. Additionally, the study addresses the pressure drop and consequently pumping power. On a separate note, it emphasizes the entropy generation, considering the specified operating conditions.

Fig. 5 shows the flow path lines colored by velocity magnitude at Re = 500 and ϕ = 0 and 0.5 %. According to the figure, the velocity decreases as the fluid flows through the secondary channels. It is noteworthy that higher velocities are found in the smaller channels and upstream of the ribs, whereas lower velocities are found in the secondary channels and downstream of the ribs. It is evident that small dead zones form in the secondary channels and minimal dead zones behind the ribs. Furthermore, the velocity magnitude at ϕ = 0.5 % exceeds that at ϕ = 0. This is because increasing the concentration leads to a higher inlet velocity at the same Re due to increased viscosity. The presence of ribs and secondary flows enhances flow mixing, disrupting the thermal boundary layers and potentially increasing heat transfer rates. Fig. 6 illustrates the impact of Re and ϕ on h. As Re values increase, h significantly intensifies. Elevated fluid velocity enhances fluid convection, leading to higher h values. Additionally, the thinner lower velocity boundary layer decreases thermal boundary layer thickness, further enhancing h. On the other hand, increasing ϕ improves h. Higher ϕ values increase thermal conductivity, enhancing thermal capacity and h. This is because h depends on the ratio of thermal conductivity to thermal boundary layer thickness. In this case, the increase in boundary layer thickness is less than the increase in thermal conductivity, resulting in increased h [44,48,49]. The h experiences a maximum increase of 24.9 % at Re = 100 and ϕ = 0.5 %. Moreover, the impact of increasing ϕ on h decreases with increasing Re. This could be due to elevated fluid momentum at higher Re values, resulting in higher h, which diminishes the impact of high nanofluid concentration. Conversely, at lower Re values, where fluid inertia is reduced, the impact of introducing nanoparticles in the base fluid becomes more prominent [3].

The temperature of the bottom surface of the MCHS, which is in direct contact with electronic chips, is crucial. Table 3 displays the average temperature on this surface across various Reynolds numbers and concentrations. The table reveals a notable decrease in the bottom surface temperature as the concentration increases. For instance, at Re = 100, the temperature drops by 3.78 K when the concentration increases from 0 to 0.5 %. Notably, at lower Re, this temperature decrease is more pronounced. This phenomenon occurs because the inertia term predominates at higher Re, while the diffusion term is stronger at lower Re, resulting in a comparatively smaller effect of nanoparticle addition on the heat transfer process [3,28]. Additionally, increasing Re leads to a substantial reduction in the bottom surface temperature, with a decrease of 13.92 K observed at ϕ = 0.5 % as Re rises from 100 to 500.

In the context of MCHSs, Tmax holds paramount importance alongside the average surface temperature. A lower Tmax indicates a diminished likelihood of hot spot generation on electronic chips, a phenomenon known to compromise the performance and longevity of electronic components. The graphical representation in Fig. 7 (a) elucidates the variation of Tmax with various Re and ϕ. It is discernible that both an increase in Re and ϕ exhibit a reduction in the Tmax. Clearly, for each Re, increasing the ϕ slightly reduces the Tmax. Additionally, at higher Re values, the Tmax significantly decreases. Consequently, the lowest Tmax is observed at ϕ of 0.5 % and Re of 500. This indicates that increasing either the ϕ or the Re reduces the overall risk of hot spot formation, with the least concern for hot spot formation being associated with a 0.5 % NF flow at Re of 500. Notably, the reduction in Tmax is more pronounced with an elevation in ϕ at lower Re values. For instance, an increase in concentration from 0 to 0.5 % results in a maximum temperature reduction of 5.88 K at Re = 100 and 1.54 K at Re = 500. A clear explanation about the effect of the Re and ϕ on the temperature distribution along the base wall is illustrated in Fig. 7 (b). Noticeably, increasing Re and ϕ lead to more uniform temperature distribution along the bottom surface. This uniform temperature can reduce the possibility of hot spot appearance and thermal failure of electronic chips.

For a clearer understanding of the impact of using GQDs NF in the considered MCHS, Fig. 7 (c) illustrates the contrast between the fluid's average temperature and its bottom surface temperature across a range of Re and ϕ. It's evident that the temperature difference consistently decreases as ϕ increases for all Re conditions. This finding underscores the superior effectiveness of the nanofluid compared to the base fluid.

When θ is at lower levels, the disparity between Tmax and Tmin is minimal. Consequently, the temperature distribution across the electronic chip becomes more uniform, indicating a reduced likelihood of thermal stress generation. Fig. 8 (a) illustrates the variation of ϴ for different values of Re and ϕ. It is evident that incorporating nanoparticles into the base fluid and increasing their ϕ reduces the θ. Additionally, higher Re values correspond to lower θ values and a more uniform temperature distribution. Increasing Re and ϕ leads to a notable decrease in ϴ. This is due to when boosting either ϕ or velocity causes a reduction in the Tmax of the MCHS. For instance, an increase in concentration from 0 to 0.5 % results in a 14.3 % reduction in ϴ at Re = 100. Moreover, the effect of using NFs is more pronounced at lower Re values, due to the greater increase in the h at lower Re values when nanoparticles are added to the base fluid.

Another crucial factor influencing the cooling effectiveness of MCHSs is the R. A lower R indicates improved heat dissipation by the coolant from the electronic chip, resulting in enhanced MCHS performance. Fig. 8 (b) illustrates the variation of R for different values of Re and ϕ. Increasing Re and ϕ results in a notable decrease in R. This can be attributed to the fact that the value of R is directly linked to Tmean of the bottom surface (Equation (8)). Consequently, the decrease in R can be caused by the reduction in Tmean. The lower the values of Tmean, the more significant the decrease in R. For example, when the concentration increases from 0 to 0.5 %, it results in a 15.82 % reduction in R at Re = 100.

Working with fluids that exhibit favorable characteristics, such as low pressure drop due to low density and viscosity, is crucial in selecting a suitable working fluid to achieve the desired performance with minimal pumping power costs. Despite NFs demonstrating improved thermal performance compared to base fluids, they also exhibit higher viscosities due to the dispersion of nanoparticles. This increase in viscosity contributes to a rise in the pressure drop. Hence, it is imperative to evaluate pressure drop and pumping power alongside thermal characteristics. In Fig. 9 (a)
the results depicted illustrate the variations in pressure drop across different Re and ϕ. It is evident that an increase in Re leads to a substantial rise in pressure drop. For example, as Re transitions from 100 to 500, the pressure drop for water increases by approximately 108.8 kPa. Moreover, increasing the concentration results in higher pressure drop, with the highest value recorded at Re = 500 and ϕ = 0.5 %. For instance, the pressure drop increases by 3.7 kPa when ϕ increases from 0 to 0.5 %. The intensification in pressure drop with higher Re is attributed to the heightened fluid velocity and thinner hydrodynamic boundary layer. At elevated Re, the fluid-solid contact region experiences a greater velocity gradient, resulting in increased shear stresses and, consequently, higher pressure drop. Additionally, the introduction of nanoscale particles to the base liquid increases the flow's viscosity, leading to higher shear stresses and pressure drop [3].

Furthermore, increase in concentration also contributes to an increase in pressure drop. As depicted in Fig. 5, velocity increases with higher ϕ, and, as previously explained, the pressure drop value correlates with this velocity increase. The pumping power values for different Re and ϕ are recorded in Fig. 9 (b). Clearly, increasing both Re and ϕ leads to an increase in pumping power across the system due to the increased pressure drop. The pumping power increased by 63.8 % when using GQDs NF compared to the base fluid under the conditions (ϕ = 0.5 %, Re = 500). Therefore, pumping power intensifies significantly at higher Re values. For a better understanding of the effect of Re and ϕ on pressure drop, the pressure distribution along the fluid domain of the MCHS is depicted in Fig. 9 (c).

Dispersing the GQDs nanoparticles in the base fluid significantly improved the heat transfer characteristics of the MCHS. However, an increase in pumping power was observed. To comprehensively assess the thermohydraulic performance of GQDs NF in the studied MCHS, PEC was introduced. The PEC indicates the advantage of nanofluids over the base fluid when its value exceeds one. In other words, for nanofluids with a PEC greater than 1, the influence of pumping power is less noticeable compared to the enhancement in heat transfer. Therefore, nanofluids with a PEC value below one are not beneficial and should not be recommended as potential heat transfer fluids. Fig. 9 (d) shows the PEC values of the GQDs NF under different Re and ϕ. The observation indicates that the PEC value increases with ϕ, but it decreases with an increase in Re. This assures that the enhancement due to the addition of nanoparticles considerably exceeds the negative effect of increased pumping power. Notably, in MCHSs, the small length scales of the fluid passages lead to a significant intensification of pressure drop as Re increases. Consequently, the microchannel being investigated exhibits higher PEC values at lower Re. Overall, it is revealed that using the NF with ϕ = 0.5 % at Re = 100 enhances thermal characteristics while experiencing a minimal increase in pressure drop. Consequently, this case is recommended for real-world applications.

This section is dedicated to highlighting the assessment of the MCHS under investigation with GQDs NF as a coolant from the perspective of the second law of thermodynamics. Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the effect of Re and ϕ on the S
h . The S
h decreases significantly with increasing Re and ϕ. For instance, at Re = 100, an increase in ϕ from 0 to 0.5 % led to a reduction in S
h by more than 14.8 %. Conversely, boosting the Re of water from 100 to 500 resulted in a decrease in S
h by 67.7 %. Essentially, the fluid's average temperature, thermal conductivity, and temperature gradient all affect the S
h . An increase in Re at a constant ϕ causes the working fluid's average temperature to drop, raising the S
h . However, this increase in Re also causes the working fluid's thermal conductivity to drop, lowering the S
h in parallel. Additionally, a rise in Re promotes fluid mixing, reducing the temperature gradient. The results shown in Fig. 10 (a) indicate that the combined effect of the decrease in temperature gradient and thermal conductivity is greater than the effect of the decrease in average temperature of the working fluid. Consequently, as Re increases, the S
h decreases. Furthermore, increasing the values of ϕ for the working fluid causes the density and viscosity to rise. Since the increase in viscosity is more pronounced than the increase in density, the working fluid's inlet velocity increases at the same Re, lowering the S
h .

Fig. 10 (b) depicts the effect of Re and ϕ on the S
f . As shown, S
f exhibits a significant increase with rising values of Re and ϕ. However, the impact of increasing Re is more pronounced than the impact of boosting ϕ. Generally, the factors influencing S
f include the coolant's velocity gradient, average temperature, and viscosity. As previously mentioned, an increase in Re leads to a decrease in the average temperature of the coolant, thereby causing an increase in S
f . Furthermore, an increase in Re results in a thinner hydrodynamic boundary layer and an augmented velocity gradient, leading to an increase in S
f . Moreover, raising ϕ at a constant Re increases the viscosity and decreases in the average coolant temperature, eventually increasing the values of S
f [44].

Fig. 10 (c) shows the effect of Re and ϕ on the S
t . As depicted, S
t significantly declines with increasing values of Re and ϕ. For instance, at Re = 100, when ϕ increases from 0 to 0.5 %, a 14.7 % decrease in S
t is recorded. Conversely, at ϕ = 0.5 %, increasing Re from 100 to 500 leads to a 59.9 % decrement in S
t . Evidently, S
t represents the combination of both S
h and S
f . The decrease in S
t is primarily attributed to the more dominant effect of decreasing S
h compared to the concurrent increase in S
f .

In Fig. 11 (a) , it is observed that the Be exhibits smaller values at higher values of ϕ. This signifies an increase in the contribution of the entropy generation due to S
f for the GQDs NF. Furthermore, Be decreases significantly as Re rises, with this reduction being more pronounced at higher values. The decline in Be indicates that within S
t , the contribution of entropy generated through S
h decreases in comparison to that of S
f . Specifically, from low to moderate Re values, S
h makes a significant contribution compared to S
f , but this contribution gradually diminishes as Re increases. In the same context, to clarify the percentage of the contribution of S
h and S
f to S
t , this contribution percentage under various operating conditions is depicted in Fig. 11(b).

The aim of the present paper is to evaluate the merit of employing a novel graphene quantum dot nanofluid in a microchannel heat sink with rectangular ribs and secondary channels for the thermal management of high heat flux electronic chips. The study examined the use of the nanofluid with particle concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.5 %, across Reynolds numbers ranging from 100 to 500. The findings indicated that, at a Reynolds number of 100, the nanofluid improved the convective heat transfer coefficient, thermal resistance, temperature uniformity, and total entropy generation by 24.9 %, 15.8 %, 14.3 %, and 14.7 %, respectively. For a Reynolds number of 500, these improvements were 18.9 %, 14.8 %, 14 %, and 3.59 %, respectively, indicating a more significant impact at lower Reynolds numbers. The performance evaluation criterion demonstrated that the nanofluid consistently improved the convective heat transfer coefficient more than the pressure drop under all operating conditions, highlighting its superior thermal and hydraulic performance. Moreover, the best combined thermohydraulic performance was observed at a particle concentration of 0.5 % and a Reynolds number of 100, which are thus recommended as the optimal working conditions.

To advance the application of the graphene quantum dot nanofluid in microchannel heat sinks, future studies should focus on several key areas. Exploring hybrid nanofluids by combining graphene quantum dot nanoparticles with other nanoparticles could enhance thermal properties and reduce pressure drop further. Furthermore, conducting multi-objective optimization studies to determine the optimal combination of Reynolds number, nanoparticle concentration, and microchannel design is essential for maximizing thermal performance while minimizing pressure drop.
This work was supported by the 2024 Yeungnam University Research Grant.

M. Mohamed Souby: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Mohamed H.S. Bargal: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hisham Maher: Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohammad Salman: Writing – review & editing. Jie Liu: Writing – review & editing. Sung Chul Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
